Overall, ChainPoll provides a practical and scalable solution for improving hallucination detection in modern LLM systems.

title: HaloScope: Harnessing Unlabeled LLM Generations for Hallucination Detection
authors: Xuefeng Du, Chaowei Xiao, Yixuan Li
text: The increasing deployment of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns about hallucinations, motivating the need for reliable detection methods. This paper introduces HaloScope, a novel framework that leverages unlabeled LLM generations in the wild for hallucination detection. The approach estimates membership of samples as truthful or hallucinated by identifying a latent subspace in LLM representations and uses this to train a binary truthfulness classifier without requiring labeled data. HaloScope eliminates the need for costly human annotations and demonstrates superior performance across multiple benchmarks, significantly improving hallucination detection accuracy compared to existing methods.

title: HaloScope: Harnessing Unlabeled LLM Generations for Hallucination Detection
authors: Xuefeng Du, Chaowei Xiao, Yixuan Li
text: This work presents HaloScope, a framework that leverages unlabeled LLM-generated data to detect hallucinations by estimating membership through latent representation subspaces and training a truthfulness classifier. The method achieves strong performance across diverse datasets and model architectures without requiring labeled data, demonstrating scalability and practicality. The findings highlight the potential of using unlabeled data for robust hallucination detection and suggest future research directions for improving generalization under distribution shifts.

title: LLM-Check: Investigating Detection of Hallucinations in Large Language Models
authors: Gaurang Sriramanan, Siddhant Bharti, Vinu Sankar Sadasivan, Shoumik Saha, Priyatham Kattakinda, Soheil Feizi
text: Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to hallucinations—outputs that are plausible yet factually incorrect. This paper investigates methods for detecting hallucinations across various real-world settings and proposes LLM-Check, a framework that analyzes a single model response using internal hidden states, attention maps, and output probabilities. Unlike prior approaches that require multiple responses or large external datasets, LLM-Check operates efficiently in both white-box and black-box settings. The proposed methods achieve significant improvements in detection performance while providing substantial computational speedups compared to existing techniques.

title: LLM-Check: Investigating Detection of Hallucinations in Large Language Models
authors: Gaurang Sriramanan, Siddhant Bharti, Vinu Sankar Sadasivan, Shoumik Saha, Priyatham Kattakinda, Soheil Feizi
text: This work presents LLM-Check, a suite of efficient hallucination detection techniques that leverage internal model representations and output probabilities to identify hallucinations within a single response. The approach demonstrates strong performance across diverse datasets and settings, including zero-resource and retrieval-based scenarios, while maintaining significantly lower computational cost than existing methods. The findings highlight the potential of internal model signals for scalable and real-time hallucination detection, offering a promising direction for improving reliability in large language models.

title: InterrogateLLM: Zero-Resource Hallucination Detection in LLM-Generated Answers
authors: Yakir Yehuda, Itzik Malkiel, Oren Barkan, Jonathan Weill, Royi Ronen, Noam Koenigstein
text: Large language models (LLMs) often generate hallucinated answers that appear plausible but lack factual grounding, limiting their reliability in real-world applications. This paper introduces InterrogateLLM, a zero-resource hallucination detection method inspired by human interrogation techniques that rely on consistency checks. The approach reconstructs queries from generated answers through repeated backward prompting and measures inconsistency between the original and reconstructed queries. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and models demonstrate that InterrogateLLM effectively detects hallucinations without requiring external knowledge, achieving strong performance and highlighting the prevalence of hallucinations in modern LLMs.

title: InterrogateLLM: Zero-Resource Hallucination Detection in LLM-Generated Answers
authors: Yakir Yehuda, Itzik Malkiel, Oren Barkan, Jonathan Weill, Royi Ronen, Noam Koenigstein
text: This work proposes InterrogateLLM, a novel method for detecting hallucinations in LLM-generated answers by leveraging query reconstruction and consistency analysis. The method demonstrates strong performance across diverse datasets and improves over existing baselines without relying on external knowledge or labeled data. While effective, limitations remain in handling ambiguous mappings and partial hallucinations, suggesting future work in extending the approach to retrieval-augmented settings and improving robustness. Overall, the study contributes to advancing reliable hallucination detection for real-world LLM applications.

title: Detecting LLM Hallucinations Using Monte Carlo Simulations on Token Probabilities
authors: Grant Ledger, Rafael Mancinni
text: Hallucinations in AI-generated content pose a significant challenge to the reliability and trustworthiness of large language models. This paper introduces a novel approach that employs Monte Carlo simulations on token probabilities to detect hallucinations. The method involves generating multiple outputs, estimating token probabilities, and using probabilistic sampling to identify low-probability tokens indicative of hallucinations. Experimental results show varying hallucination rates across domains, with everyday scenarios being the most challenging. The proposed probabilistic framework enhances the analysis of model outputs and improves the detection of unreliable or fabricated content.

title: Detecting LLM Hallucinations Using Monte Carlo Simulations on Token Probabilities
authors: Grant Ledger, Rafael Mancinni
text: This study demonstrates that Monte Carlo simulations on token probabilities provide an effective framework for detecting hallucinations in large language models. By analyzing probability distributions and identifying low-probability tokens, the approach enables robust detection of both subtle and overt hallucinations. The findings highlight variability in hallucination rates across domains and emphasize the need for adaptive and context-aware detection methods. Overall, the probabilistic methodology contributes to improving the reliability and trustworthiness of AI-generated content and supports future advancements in hallucination mitigation.

title: FENICE: Factuality Evaluation of Summarization Based on Natural Language Inference and Claim Extraction
authors: Alessandro Scirè, Karim Ghonim, Roberto Navigli
text: Recent advancements in text summarization with large language models have improved performance but continue to suffer from factual inconsistencies such as hallucinations. Existing factuality metrics face limitations including lack of interpretability, focus on short texts, and high computational cost. This paper introduces FENICE, a factuality evaluation metric that leverages natural language inference and claim extraction to assess consistency between summaries and source documents. By aligning extracted claims with supporting evidence in the source text, FENICE provides interpretable and efficient evaluation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the AGGREFACT benchmark and demonstrating effectiveness on long-form summarization tasks.

title: FENICE: Factuality Evaluation of Summarization Based on Natural Language Inference and Claim Extraction
authors: Alessandro Scirè, Karim Ghonim, Roberto Navigli
text: This work introduces FENICE, a novel and interpretable factuality evaluation metric for summarization that combines claim extraction with natural language inference-based alignment across multiple text granularities. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks and demonstrates strong effectiveness on long-form summarization through human-annotated evaluations. By improving interpretability and computational efficiency, FENICE provides a practical solution for assessing factual consistency in generated summaries and highlights directions for future improvements in alignment and multilingual evaluation.

title: Generating Benchmarks for Factuality Evaluation of Language Models
authors: Dor Muhlgay, Ori Ram, Inbal Magar, Yoav Levine, Nir Ratner, Yonatan Belinkov, Omri Abend, Kevin Leyton-Brown, Amnon Shashua, Yoav Shoham
text: Evaluating the factuality of language models is essential for safe deployment, yet existing methods often rely on model-generated facts and fail to control the evaluation space. This paper introduces FACTOR (Factual Assessment via Corpus TransfORmation), a scalable framework that converts factual corpora into benchmarks by generating contrastive examples with both true and systematically perturbed false statements. The approach enables controlled evaluation of factual knowledge across domains. Experiments on Wiki-FACTOR, News-FACTOR, and Expert-FACTOR demonstrate that factuality improves with model size and retrieval augmentation, and that FACTOR scores better reflect factuality in open-ended generation than perplexity.

title: Generating Benchmarks for Factuality Evaluation of Language Models
authors: Dor Muhlgay, Ori Ram, Inbal Magar, Yoav Levine, Nir Ratner, Yonatan Belinkov, Omri Abend, Kevin Leyton-Brown, Amnon Shashua, Yoav Shoham
text: This work presents FACTOR, a framework for constructing benchmarks that evaluate language model factuality through contrastive examples of true and false statements. The results show that FACTOR provides a complementary and more reliable measure of factuality compared to perplexity and aligns better with human judgments in open-ended generation. The study highlights the importance of controlled evaluation datasets and negative examples for assessing factual knowledge, and suggests future work in extending FACTOR to new domains and improving benchmark generation processes.

title: NonFactS: NonFactual Summary Generation for Factuality Evaluation in Document Summarization
authors: Amir Soleimani, Christof Monz, Marcel Worring
text: Pre-trained abstractive summarization models often generate fluent but factually inconsistent summaries. To address the lack of large-scale training data for factuality evaluation, this paper introduces NonFactS, a data generation model that synthesizes nonfactual summaries from source documents and reference summaries. The generated samples are more abstractive and closely resemble factual summaries, enabling improved training of document-level entailment classifiers. Experiments show that NonFactS achieves state-of-the-art performance on factuality benchmarks such as FALSESUM and SUMMAC and can generalize even without human-annotated summaries.

title: NonFactS: NonFactual Summary Generation for Factuality Evaluation in Document Summarization
authors: Amir Soleimani, Christof Monz, Marcel Worring
text: This work presents NonFactS, a method for generating large-scale nonfactual summaries to support factuality evaluation in document summarization. By producing realistic and abstractive nonfactual samples, the approach enables effective training of classifiers that outperform prior methods on standard benchmarks. The method also demonstrates strong performance without requiring human-annotated reference summaries, highlighting its practicality for new domains. Future work includes addressing limitations related to noisy and ungrammatical summaries and improving robustness against surface-level biases.

title: Large Language Models Hallucination: A Comprehensive Survey
authors: Aisha Alansari, Hamzah Luqman
text: Large language models have achieved strong performance across natural language processing tasks but often generate hallucinated content that is fluent yet factually incorrect. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of hallucination in LLMs, including its causes, detection methods, and mitigation strategies. It introduces taxonomies for hallucination types, detection approaches, and mitigation techniques, and analyzes their strengths and limitations. The survey also reviews benchmarks and evaluation metrics, and highlights open challenges and future research directions for improving the reliability and trustworthiness of LLMs.

title: Large Language Models Hallucination: A Comprehensive Survey
authors: Aisha Alansari, Hamzah Luqman
text: This survey presents a comprehensive analysis of hallucination in large language models, covering its causes across the development lifecycle, as well as detection and mitigation techniques. It emphasizes that no single method is sufficient to address hallucinations and that combining complementary approaches such as retrieval, uncertainty estimation, and reasoning is essential. The study highlights limitations in current benchmarks and evaluation metrics and identifies key challenges, including multilingual settings and reasoning complexity. It concludes by outlining future directions for developing more robust, trustworthy, and factually accurate language models.

title: Towards Faithful Model Explanation in NLP: A Survey
authors: Qing Lyu, Marianna Apidianaki, Chris Callison-Burch
text: Neural NLP models achieve strong performance but lack transparency, motivating research on explainability. This survey reviews over 110 model explanation methods through the lens of faithfulness, defined as how accurately an explanation reflects a model’s reasoning process. The paper discusses definitions and evaluation of faithfulness, categorizes explanation methods into five groups, and analyzes their strengths and limitations. It highlights challenges in evaluating faithfulness and provides insights for developing more reliable and interpretable NLP models.

title: Towards Faithful Model Explanation in NLP: A Survey
authors: Qing Lyu, Marianna Apidianaki, Chris Callison-Burch
text: This survey examines model explanation methods in NLP with a focus on faithfulness, emphasizing that explanations must accurately reflect the underlying reasoning of models. It highlights that current evaluation methods for faithfulness are inconsistent and often rely on problematic assumptions, making comparisons difficult. The paper identifies key challenges, including balancing faithfulness and plausibility, and calls for more standardized evaluation frameworks and improved methodologies to ensure reliable and trustworthy explanations in NLP systems.

title: Towards Fine-Grained Citation Evaluation in Generated Text: A Comparative Analysis of Faithfulness Metrics
authors: Weijia Zhang, Mohammad Aliannejadi, Yifei Yuan, Jiahuan Pei, Jia-Hong Huang, Evangelos Kanoulas
text: Large language models often generate hallucinated content, and retrieval-augmented models address this by incorporating citations. However, evaluating how well citations support generated statements remains challenging, especially beyond binary classification. This paper proposes a comparative evaluation framework for fine-grained citation support, distinguishing full, partial, and no support. The framework uses correlation, classification, and retrieval evaluations to assess alignment between faithfulness metrics and human judgments. Results show that no single metric consistently performs best across all settings, highlighting the complexity of fine-grained citation evaluation.

title: Towards Fine-Grained Citation Evaluation in Generated Text: A Comparative Analysis of Faithfulness Metrics
authors: Weijia Zhang, Mohammad Aliannejadi, Yifei Yuan, Jiahuan Pei, Jia-Hong Huang, Evangelos Kanoulas
text: This work introduces a framework for evaluating faithfulness metrics in fine-grained citation support scenarios, demonstrating that existing metrics struggle to consistently distinguish between full, partial, and no support. The findings highlight that different evaluation protocols are complementary and necessary for comprehensive assessment. The study emphasizes the need for improved training resources, more robust metric design, and explainable evaluation methods to better capture nuanced citation support in retrieval-augmented language model outputs.

title: Grounding and Evaluation for Large Language Models: Practical Challenges and Lessons Learned
authors: Krishnaram Kenthapadi, Mehrnoosh Sameki, Ankur Taly
text: The rapid adoption of large language models in high-stakes applications requires robust evaluation and grounding to ensure trustworthiness, safety, and reliability. This survey reviews challenges such as hallucinations, harmful content, bias, and security risks in generative AI systems. It outlines evaluation dimensions including truthfulness, robustness, fairness, and interpretability, and discusses practical approaches for detecting and mitigating these issues. The work highlights grounding as a key requirement for aligning model outputs with trusted knowledge sources and identifies open challenges in deploying reliable large language model systems.

title: Grounding and Evaluation for Large Language Models: Practical Challenges and Lessons Learned
authors: Krishnaram Kenthapadi, Mehrnoosh Sameki, Ankur Taly
text: This survey emphasizes the importance of integrating responsible AI practices into the development and deployment of large language models, focusing on grounding, evaluation, and mitigation of harms such as hallucinations and bias. It highlights the need for comprehensive evaluation frameworks that consider multiple dimensions including safety, robustness, and transparency. The study underscores open challenges in grounding, monitoring, and real-world deployment, and calls for continued research to build more trustworthy and reliable generative AI systems.

title: Provenance: A Light-weight Fact-checker for Retrieval Augmented LLM Generation Output
authors: Hithesh Sankararaman, Mohammed Nasheed Yasin, Tanner Sorensen, Alessandro Di Bari, Andreas Stolcke
text: Large language models often generate nonfactual or hallucinated outputs, necessitating efficient fact-checking methods. This paper introduces Provenance, a lightweight approach for detecting nonfactual outputs in retrieval-augmented generation systems. The method uses compact natural language inference models instead of large language models to compute factuality scores based on context and generated output. Provenance achieves strong performance across multiple datasets while maintaining low latency and computational cost, enabling scalable and interpretable fact-checking.

title: Provenance: A Light-weight Fact-checker for Retrieval Augmented LLM Generation Output
authors: Hithesh Sankararaman, Mohammed Nasheed Yasin, Tanner Sorensen, Alessandro Di Bari, Andreas Stolcke
text: This work presents Provenance, a lightweight fact-checking framework for retrieval-augmented LLM outputs that leverages cross-encoder models for relevance scoring and natural language inference. The method demonstrates effective hallucination detection across diverse datasets while maintaining significantly lower computational cost compared to large language model-based approaches. By incorporating query-aware factuality scoring and scalable architecture, Provenance contributes to improving the reliability and trustworthiness of LLM-generated content.

title: ProveRAG: Provenance-Driven Vulnerability Analysis With Automated Retrieval-Augmented LLMs
authors: Reza Fayyazi, Stella Hoyos Trueba, Michael Zuzak, Shanchieh Jay Yang
text: Large language models are increasingly used in cybersecurity but face challenges such as hallucination, outdated knowledge, and limited context windows. This paper introduces ProveRAG, a retrieval-augmented system that integrates automated web data retrieval with self-evaluation to improve vulnerability analysis. The system incorporates a provenance mechanism that links generated outputs to verifiable sources such as NVD and CWE, along with a self-critique process to detect hallucinations and omissions. Experimental results show high accuracy in generating exploitation and mitigation strategies while providing verifiable evidence to support decisions.

title: ProveRAG: Provenance-Driven Vulnerability Analysis With Automated Retrieval-Augmented LLMs
authors: Reza Fayyazi, Stella Hoyos Trueba, Michael Zuzak, Shanchieh Jay Yang
text: This work presents ProveRAG, a system that enhances large language model performance in cybersecurity by combining retrieval augmentation, summarization, and self-critique mechanisms. The approach mitigates hallucination and omission errors while providing verifiable evidence and provenance for generated outputs. By integrating authoritative sources and structured reasoning, ProveRAG improves the reliability, transparency, and auditability of vulnerability analysis, demonstrating strong performance across models and real-world scenarios.

title: Semantic Grounding Index: Geometric Bounds on Context Engagement in RAG Systems
authors: Javier Marin
text: Retrieval-augmented generation systems can hallucinate when responses fail to engage with retrieved context. This paper introduces the Semantic Grounding Index (SGI), a geometric metric based on angular distances in embedding space to measure the relationship between a response, its query, and the retrieved context. The study shows that hallucinated responses remain closer to the query than the context, a phenomenon termed semantic laziness. Experiments demonstrate that SGI effectively distinguishes grounded from ungrounded responses across multiple embedding models, while highlighting its limitation in capturing factual accuracy rather than contextual engagement.

title: Semantic Grounding Index: Geometric Bounds on Context Engagement in RAG Systems
authors: Javier Marin
text: This work introduces the Semantic Grounding Index as a theoretically grounded and computationally efficient method for detecting context disengagement in retrieval-augmented generation systems. The results show that hallucinated responses exhibit semantic laziness, remaining closer to the query than the retrieved context in embedding space. SGI demonstrates strong robustness across models and predictable behavior based on geometric properties, while its limitations highlight that it measures contextual engagement rather than factual correctness. The study provides practical guidance for deploying SGI alongside complementary methods for reliable hallucination detection.

title: GaRAGe: A Benchmark with Grounding Annotations for RAG Evaluation
authors: Ionut-Teodor Sorodoc, Leonardo F. R. Ribeiro, Rexhina Blloshmi, Christopher Davis, Adrià de Gispert
text: We introduce GaRAGe, a large benchmark for evaluating retrieval-augmented generation systems with human-curated long-form answers and fine-grained grounding annotations. The dataset includes 2366 questions and over 35,000 annotated passages from both private knowledge bases and the Web, enabling evaluation of whether models correctly identify and use relevant grounding. Experiments show that current large language models often over-summarize instead of grounding responses strictly in relevant passages and struggle to deflect when grounding is insufficient.

title: GaRAGe: A Benchmark with Grounding Annotations for RAG Evaluation
authors: Ionut-Teodor Sorodoc, Leonardo F. R. Ribeiro, Rexhina Blloshmi, Christopher Davis, Adrià de Gispert
text: This work presents GaRAGe, a comprehensive benchmark with grounding annotations that enables fine-grained evaluation of retrieval-augmented generation systems. The results reveal that both proprietary and open-source models struggle to distinguish relevant from irrelevant grounding, often relying on summarization rather than precise attribution. The benchmark introduces relevance-aware factuality metrics and highlights key challenges such as handling noisy grounding, time-sensitive questions, and deflection behavior, providing a foundation for improving grounded generation in real-world applications.

title: ORION Grounded in Context: Retrieval-Based Method for Hallucination Detection
authors: Assaf Gerner, Netta Madvil, Nadav Barak, Alex Zaikman, Jonatan Liberman, Liron Hamra, Rotem Brazilay, Shay Tsadok, Yaron Friedman, Neal Harow, Noam Bressler, Shir Chorev, Philip Tannor
text: Hallucinations remain a significant challenge in large language model applications despite advances in retrieval-augmented generation. This paper presents Grounded in Context, a lightweight retrieval-based hallucination detection framework within the ORION family. The method decomposes outputs into factual statements, retrieves relevant context for each claim, and evaluates factual consistency using natural language inference models. It achieves strong performance on the RAGTruth benchmark, matching or surpassing comparable approaches while maintaining efficiency and scalability for long-context scenarios.

title: ORION Grounded in Context: Retrieval-Based Method for Hallucination Detection
authors: Assaf Gerner, Netta Madvil, Nadav Barak, Alex Zaikman, Jonatan Liberman, Liron Hamra, Rotem Brazilay, Shay Tsadok, Yaron Friedman, Neal Harow, Noam Bressler, Shir Chorev, Philip Tannor
text: This work introduces Grounded in Context, a retrieval-based hallucination detection method that combines claim decomposition, targeted context retrieval, and natural language inference scoring. The approach effectively addresses challenges of long context and varying statement types, demonstrating strong performance on hallucination detection benchmarks without requiring large models or dataset-specific training. The results highlight the effectiveness of proposition-level analysis and suggest future improvements through enhanced long-context encoder models and retrieval strategies.

title: Reducing hallucination in structured outputs via Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Patrice Béchard, Orlando Marquez Ayala
text: Large language models often hallucinate, limiting their reliability in real-world applications. This paper presents a retrieval-augmented generation system for producing structured outputs, specifically workflows represented as JSON. By retrieving relevant structured elements such as steps and tables and incorporating them into the model prompt, the approach significantly reduces hallucination and improves generalization to out-of-domain scenarios. The method also enables the use of smaller models without sacrificing performance, making deployment more efficient.

title: Reducing hallucination in structured outputs via Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Patrice Béchard, Orlando Marquez Ayala
text: This work demonstrates that retrieval-augmented generation can effectively reduce hallucination in structured output tasks by grounding generation in retrieved elements. The approach enables improved trustworthiness and generalization while allowing the deployment of smaller, resource-efficient models. Results highlight the importance of retrieval quality and the synergy between retriever and generator components, suggesting future work in joint optimization to further enhance performance.

title: ReDeEP: Detecting Hallucination in Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Mechanistic Interpretability
authors: Zhongxiang Sun, Xiaoxue Zang, Kai Zheng, Jun Xu, Xiao Zhang, Weijie Yu, Yang Song, Han Li
text: Retrieval-augmented generation models aim to reduce hallucinations by incorporating external knowledge, but hallucinations still occur due to conflicts between retrieved context and parametric knowledge. This paper investigates the internal mechanisms behind such hallucinations using mechanistic interpretability. It identifies that hallucinations arise when feed-forward networks overemphasize parametric knowledge while attention-based copying mechanisms fail to properly utilize external context. Based on these insights, the authors propose ReDeEP, a method that detects hallucinations by decoupling external context usage and parametric knowledge, achieving improved detection performance.

title: ReDeEP: Detecting Hallucination in Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Mechanistic Interpretability
authors: Zhongxiang Sun, Xiaoxue Zang, Kai Zheng, Jun Xu, Xiao Zhang, Weijie Yu, Yang Song, Han Li
text: This work introduces ReDeEP, a hallucination detection method that leverages mechanistic interpretability to analyze how large language models balance external context and parametric knowledge. The findings show that hallucinations are caused by insufficient use of retrieved context and excessive reliance on internal knowledge. By decoupling these factors, ReDeEP improves detection accuracy and provides a principled framework for understanding hallucinations in retrieval-augmented generation systems, offering both detection and mitigation strategies.

title: MultiRAG: A Knowledge-guided Framework for Mitigating Hallucination in Multi-source Retrieval Augmented Generation
authors: Wenlong Wu, Haofen Wang, Bohan Li, Peixuan Huang, Xinzhe Zhao, Lei Liang
text: Retrieval-augmented generation can reduce hallucinations, but integrating multiple data sources introduces challenges such as sparse data distribution and inconsistencies between sources. This paper proposes MultiRAG, a knowledge-guided framework that mitigates hallucination in multi-source settings through two key components: a knowledge construction module using multi-source line graphs to aggregate logical relationships, and a retrieval module with multi-level confidence calculation to filter unreliable information. Experiments show that MultiRAG significantly improves retrieval reliability and reduces hallucination across multi-domain and multi-hop tasks.

title: MultiRAG: A Knowledge-guided Framework for Mitigating Hallucination in Multi-source Retrieval Augmented Generation
authors: Wenlong Wu, Haofen Wang, Bohan Li, Peixuan Huang, Xinzhe Zhao, Lei Liang
text: This work introduces MultiRAG, a framework for mitigating hallucination in multi-source retrieval-augmented generation by combining knowledge aggregation and multi-level confidence filtering. The approach effectively handles challenges such as sparse data distribution and inter-source inconsistencies, improving retrieval accuracy and reducing hallucinations. Experimental results demonstrate robust performance gains and highlight the importance of structured knowledge integration and confidence-aware filtering for reliable multi-source reasoning.

title: Rowen: Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Hallucination Mitigation in LLMs
authors: Hanxing Ding, Liang Pang, Zihao Wei, Huawei Shen, Xueqi Cheng
text: Large language models often generate hallucinated outputs due to limitations in their parametric knowledge and the introduction of irrelevant external information during retrieval. This paper proposes Rowen, an adaptive retrieval-augmented generation framework that balances internal reasoning and external knowledge usage. It introduces a consistency-based hallucination detection module that evaluates semantic inconsistencies across responses generated in different languages or models, triggering retrieval only when necessary. Experimental results show that Rowen outperforms existing methods in both detecting and mitigating hallucinations while improving retrieval efficiency.

title: Rowen: Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Hallucination Mitigation in LLMs
authors: Hanxing Ding, Liang Pang, Zihao Wei, Huawei Shen, Xueqi Cheng
text: This work presents Rowen, an adaptive framework that mitigates hallucinations in large language models by selectively integrating retrieval augmentation based on consistency-based uncertainty detection. By balancing parametric knowledge and external evidence, Rowen effectively reduces both internal and external hallucinations while minimizing unnecessary retrieval operations. Experimental results demonstrate significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting the importance of adaptive retrieval strategies for reliable and efficient generation.

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
authors: Junyi Li, Xiaoxue Cheng, Wayne Xin Zhao, Jian-Yun Nie, Ji-Rong Wen
text: Large language models are prone to hallucinations, generating content that conflicts with sources or cannot be verified. This paper introduces HaluEval, a large-scale benchmark of 35,000 human-annotated and automatically generated hallucinated samples for evaluating LLMs’ ability to recognize hallucinations. The dataset is constructed using a sampling-then-filtering framework combined with human annotation. Experiments show that models like ChatGPT frequently produce hallucinated content and struggle to detect it, while external knowledge and reasoning can improve recognition performance.

title: HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models
authors: Junyi Li, Xiaoxue Cheng, Wayne Xin Zhao, Jian-Yun Nie, Ji-Rong Wen
text: This work presents HaluEval, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating hallucination recognition in large language models. The results reveal that existing models frequently generate hallucinated content and have difficulty identifying such errors. The study highlights that incorporating external knowledge and reasoning strategies can improve hallucination detection. HaluEval provides a valuable resource for analyzing hallucination patterns and advancing the development of more reliable language models.

title: Evaluating Open-Domain Question Answering in the Era of Large Language Models
authors: Ehsan Kamalloo, Nouha Dziri, Charles L. A. Clarke, Davood Rafiei
text: Lexical matching is the standard evaluation method for open-domain question answering, but it fails when plausible answers are not included in gold annotations, especially for generative large language models. This paper analyzes open-domain QA evaluation by manually assessing model outputs and shows that true performance is significantly underestimated. The study finds that many evaluation failures arise from semantically equivalent answers and that automated evaluation models struggle to detect hallucinations in long-form LLM outputs, making human evaluation necessary.

title: Evaluating Open-Domain Question Answering in the Era of Large Language Models
authors: Ehsan Kamalloo, Nouha Dziri, Charles L. A. Clarke, Davood Rafiei
text: This work demonstrates that traditional lexical matching metrics are insufficient for evaluating open-domain question answering in the era of large language models, as they underestimate performance and fail to account for semantic equivalence. While alternative automated evaluation methods provide partial improvements, they still struggle with hallucinated and long-form answers. The study highlights the continued necessity of human evaluation and calls for improved evaluation techniques that better capture correctness and factuality in LLM-generated responses.

title: IQA-EVAL: Automatic Evaluation of Human-Model Interactive Question Answering
authors: Ruosen Li, Ruochen Li, Barry Wang, Xinya Du
text: Traditional evaluation of large language models for question answering focuses on single-turn responses and fails to capture interactive human-AI dynamics. This paper introduces IQA-EVAL, an automatic evaluation framework for interactive question answering using LLM-based evaluation agents that simulate human behaviors and assess interactions. The framework incorporates personas to better reflect diverse user preferences and achieves strong correlation with human evaluations while significantly reducing evaluation cost.

title: IQA-EVAL: Automatic Evaluation of Human-Model Interactive Question Answering
authors: Ruosen Li, Ruochen Li, Barry Wang, Xinya Du
text: This work presents IQA-EVAL, a framework for automatically evaluating interactive question answering systems using LLM-based agents for both interaction generation and evaluation. By incorporating persona-based simulations, the approach better captures diverse user behaviors and aligns closely with human judgments. The results demonstrate that IQA-EVAL provides a scalable and cost-effective alternative to human evaluation while offering comprehensive insights into the performance of modern language models in interactive settings.

title: Evaluating Open-QA Evaluation
authors: Cunxiang Wang, Sirui Cheng, Qipeng Guo, Yuanhao Yue, Bowen Ding, Zhikun Xu, Yidong Wang, Xiangkun Hu, Zheng Zhang, Yue Zhang
text: This paper studies the evaluation of open-domain question answering as a means to assess the factuality of large language models. It highlights the limitations of existing automatic evaluation methods and introduces a new task, QA-Eval, along with the EVOUNA dataset, which uses human annotations to benchmark evaluators. Experiments show that current metrics, including lexical matching and neural methods, fall short of human evaluation, while LLM-based evaluators such as GPT-4 show promising but still imperfect performance.

title: Evaluating Open-QA Evaluation
authors: Cunxiang Wang, Sirui Cheng, Qipeng Guo, Yuanhao Yue, Bowen Ding, Zhikun Xu, Yidong Wang, Xiangkun Hu, Zheng Zhang, Yue Zhang
text: This work introduces the EVOUNA dataset and QA-Eval task to systematically evaluate open-domain question answering evaluation methods. The results show that existing evaluators, including lexical matching, neural metrics, and LLM-based approaches, all exhibit limitations compared to human judgment. While GPT-4 demonstrates strong performance among automatic methods, human evaluation remains the most reliable standard. The findings provide insights into improving automatic evaluation techniques for factuality assessment in large language models.

title: Evaluating Retrieval Quality in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Alireza Salemi, Hamed Zamani
text: Evaluating retrieval-augmented generation systems is challenging, especially for assessing retrieval quality. Traditional end-to-end evaluation is computationally expensive and has weak correlation with downstream performance. This paper proposes eRAG, a method that evaluates each retrieved document by using the language model to generate outputs and scoring them against downstream task metrics. Experiments show that eRAG achieves significantly higher correlation with downstream performance while being more computationally efficient.

title: Evaluating Retrieval Quality in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Alireza Salemi, Hamed Zamani
text: This work introduces eRAG, a novel approach for evaluating retrieval models in retrieval-augmented generation by leveraging document-level contributions to downstream task performance. The method demonstrates higher correlation with overall system performance compared to traditional evaluation techniques and significantly improves efficiency in terms of runtime and memory usage. The findings suggest that evaluating retrieval through its impact on generation is a more effective strategy for improving RAG systems.

title: A Systematic Evaluation on Leading Large Language Models and Their Factuality Investigation as Question Answering Systems
authors: Shen Zheng
text: This thesis provides a systematic evaluation of leading large language models, focusing on their capabilities and factuality in question answering systems. It introduces GPT-Fathom, an evaluation framework that assesses over ten models across multiple benchmarks and capability categories under consistent settings. The study analyzes model evolution, identifies factors influencing performance improvements, and highlights factuality as a key limitation, emphasizing the need for better knowledge integration and evaluation methodologies.

title: A Systematic Evaluation on Leading Large Language Models and Their Factuality Investigation as Question Answering Systems
authors: Shen Zheng
text: This work presents a comprehensive evaluation of large language models and their factual reliability in question answering tasks, highlighting both their advancements and limitations. The findings show that while modern LLMs achieve strong performance across diverse benchmarks, factuality remains a significant challenge due to issues such as knowledge recall and reasoning errors. The study underscores the importance of systematic evaluation frameworks and suggests that integrating external knowledge sources and improving retrieval mechanisms are key to enhancing the reliability and trustworthiness of LLM outputs.
